metadata.title: The band gap width is just one Fourier coefficient
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=surviving states/bonding; CRIMSON=forbidden/gap region; GOLD=zone boundary marker
metadata.note: Nearly-free electron band gap = 2|V1|. Fourier coefficient of periodic potential selects the coupling.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A crystal creates a forbidden zone in the electron's energy spectrum. That zone — the band gap — is not a complicated many-body result. Its width is a single number from a Fourier table.

card.headline:
The band gap width is
just one Fourier coefficient

beats.narration_text: Free electrons in a metal have a smooth parabolic energy-momentum curve. Add a crystal lattice and a forbidden gap appears at the Brillouin zone boundary. The gap should depend on all the complicated details of the lattice. Here it depends on only one number. Why?

card.headline:
The band gap should depend
on the whole lattice.
It only needs one number.
Why?

beats.narration_text: Start with free electrons: a parabola. E equals hbar squared k squared over 2m. The parabola repeats — shifted by 2 pi over a, the reciprocal lattice spacing — giving a family of curves. At the Brillouin zone boundary, k equals pi over a, two of these parabolas cross. Two free-electron states with the same energy.

The periodic crystal potential can be expanded in a Fourier series. Each Fourier component V-sub-n corresponds to a spatial frequency of n cycles per lattice constant. The component V-sub-1 oscillates exactly once per unit cell — it matches the spacing between the degenerate pair at the zone boundary.

A periodic potential scatters an electron from momentum k to k plus a reciprocal lattice vector G. Only the Fourier component of V at that spatial frequency matters. At the first zone boundary, only V-sub-1 mixes the degenerate pair. That is the only coupling that matters.

Write the 2-by-2 matrix in the degenerate subspace. Diagonal: the free-electron energies. Off-diagonal: V-sub-1. Diagonalize it: the two eigenvalues are the free energy plus or minus the magnitude of V-sub-1. The gap between them is exactly 2 times the absolute value of V-sub-1.

The parabola develops a kink at the zone boundary. The two curves that would have crossed are pushed apart — a gap of 2 V-sub-1 opens. States inside the gap are forbidden. No electron energy can sit in that range — and the width is a table entry, not a calculation.

Higher Brillouin zone boundaries are governed by V-sub-2, V-sub-3, and so on. Change V-sub-1 and only the first gap changes. The Fourier decomposition selects, by matching spatial frequency, exactly which coefficient controls which gap.

A 1D crystal with lattice spacing 0.3 nanometers has V-sub-1 equal to minus 0.4 electron-volts. The first energy gap is 2 times 0.4 = 0.8 electron-volts wide. A student can state the gap before opening any band theory textbook. The answer lives in the Fourier table of the potential.
Periodic potential — Fourier expansion. Degenerate states at zone boundary — mixed by V-sub-1. Gap width equals 2 times the absolute value of V-sub-1. One coefficient, one gap.

card.headline:
Zone boundary degeneracy.
Mixed by V₁.
Gap = 2|V₁|.
